coredata.dc:title: Luminescent carbon dots encapsulating in Eu3+ doped gahnite spinel nanocomposite for boosting thermal sensing, advanced level III detection and intelligent anti-counterfeiting applications
coredata.dc:creator: Gagana, M.
Krushna, B.R. Radha
Sharma, S.C.
J Salwe, Kartik
George, Augustin
Sanjana, M.R.

Archana, K.V.
pasha, Sardar
Manjunatha, K.
Yun Wu, Sheng
Nagabhushana, H.
coredata.dc:description: The exceptional optical properties of carbon dots (CDs) position them as a highly promising category of multifunctional carbon-based nanomaterials. When hydrothermally prepared CDs are incorporated into europium-doped zinc aluminate ZnAl2O4 nanophosphors (ZAO:Eu3+ NPs) synthesized via ultrasonication method, the resulting CDs@ZAO:Eu3+ nanocomposite (NCs) exhibits a broad excitation band, with a prominent peak at approximately 393 nm, perfectly matching the emission wavelength range (500–750 nm) of near-ultraviolet (n-UV) LED chips. Significantly, the CDs (5 wt %)@ZAO:Eu3+ NCs exhibit a remarkable 12.5 folds enhancement in photoluminescence (PL) intensity compared to ZAO:Eu3+ alone, thanks to the Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) between CDs and Eu3+ ions. This enhancement in luminescence could stem from the CDs capturing electrons and facilitating energy transfer to the luminescent Eu3+ ions. Utilizing CDs to enhance fluorescence presents a straightforward and eco-friendly approach for boosting luminescence properties. Impressively, even under elevated temperatures reaching 420 K, the thermal stability of CDs (5 wt %)@ZAO:Eu3+ NCs remains outstanding, retaining 90 % of its initial luminescence intensity. This highlights its exceptional thermal stability, characterized by a high activation energy of 0.27 eV. With an impressive internal quantum efficiency (IQE) of 87.01 %, the optimized composite also achieves remarkable colour purity (CP), reaching 99.5 %. The relative sensitivity was 1.36 %, at 300 K. Thus, CDs@ZAO:Eu3+ is a promising material for non-contact optical thermometry applications. Utilizing the optimized NCs through a simple powder dusting technique effectively develops latent fingerprints (LFPs) on various surfaces, revealing fingerprints (FPs) under 365 nm UV light. Additionally, the study investigates diverse pore parameters including number, position, size, shape, and inter-spacing. Furthermore, optimized NCs are utilized to create a transparent anti-counterfeiting (AC) ink, offering effective AC features through its red emission under 365 nm UV light.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The exceptional optical properties of carbon dots (CDs) position them as a highly promising category of multifunctional carbon-based nanomaterials. When hydrothermally prepared CDs are incorporated into europium-doped zinc aluminate ZnAl2O4 nanophosphors (ZAO:Eu3+ NPs) synthesized via ultrasonication method, the resulting CDs@ZAO:Eu3+ nanocomposite (NCs) exhibits a broad excitation band, with a prominent peak at approximately 393 nm, perfectly matching the emission wavelength range (500–750 nm) of near-ultraviolet (n-UV) LED chips. Significantly, the CDs (5 wt %)@ZAO:Eu3+ NCs exhibit a remarkable 12.5 folds enhancement in photoluminescence (PL) intensity compared to ZAO:Eu3+ alone, thanks to the Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) between CDs and Eu3+ ions. This enhancement in luminescence could stem from the CDs capturing electrons and facilitating energy transfer to the luminescent Eu3+ ions. Utilizing CDs to enhance fluorescence presents a straightforward and eco-friendly approach for boosting luminescence properties. Impressively, even under elevated temperatures reaching 420 K, the thermal stability of CDs (5 wt %)@ZAO:Eu3+ NCs remains outstanding, retaining 90 % of its initial luminescence intensity. This highlights its exceptional thermal stability, characterized by a high activation energy of 0.27 eV. With an impressive internal quantum efficiency (IQE) of 87.01 %, the optimized composite also achieves remarkable colour purity (CP), reaching 99.5 %. The relative sensitivity was 1.36 %, at 300 K. Thus, CDs@ZAO:Eu3+ is a promising material for non-contact optical thermometry applications. Utilizing the optimized NCs through a simple powder dusting technique effectively develops latent fingerprints (LFPs) on various surfaces, revealing fingerprints (FPs) under 365 nm UV light. Additionally, the study investigates diverse pore parameters including number, position, size, shape, and inter-spacing. Furthermore, optimized NCs are utilized to create a transparent anti-counterfeiting (AC) ink, offering effective AC features through its red emission under 365 nm UV light.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Phosphors are photoluminescent materials with a wide range of applications, including temperature sensing, forensic science, and AC. Currently, nano-thermometers are gaining prominence in research, technology, healthcare, and commercial sectors due to their distinctive spectral properties and high chemical and physical stability [1–3]. Rare-earth (RE) doped inorganic nanoparticles are highly appealing for temperature monitoring across a wide temperature range. Common spectroscopic metrics used for luminescence thermometers include the full width at half maximum (FWHM) and emission lifetime, which are temperature-dependent [4,5]. In forensic science, LFPs have traditionally been discovered using methods such as ninhydrin, cyanoacrylate fuming, and powder dusting. Among these, powder dusting has gained popularity due to its simplicity, reliability, and ease of use [6]. However, traditional powders like metal and magnetic powders have been increasingly replaced by RE doped phosphors. These phosphors provide better contrast, higher selectivity, and reduced background activity, making them the preferred choice over traditional powders [7].

In general, lanthanide ion-doped nanomaterials often exhibit lower luminescence quantum efficiency due to factors such as surface defects, low molar absorptivity, and narrow absorption bands [8,9]. The luminescence properties of RE doped aluminate nanostructures are particularly affected by surface defects due to their nanoscale dimensions [10]. Viable approaches to improve luminescence performance include optimizing synthesis parameters, configuring core-shell structures, surface modification, and adding flux. Additionally, charge compensation, sensitizer incorporation, and energy transfer techniques are also preferred for enhancing the luminescence of lanthanide ion-doped nanomaterials [11–15]. To address these issues, it is essential to improve surface defects and luminescence quantum efficiency. Carbon dots can serve as sensitizers and surface modifiers, enhancing PL by tuning the optical band gap of the target material particles. CDs have become popular due to their exceptional qualities, including low toxicity, high reliability, and intense brightness [16–18]. This has led to their increasing use in various exciting applications. Incorporating CDs into phosphors enhances their morphology and luminous efficiency.

Eu3+ doped alkaline earth aluminates, such as ZnAl2O4:Eu3+ (ZAO:Eu3+), have garnered significant attention as luminescent materials for optical thermometry [19]. This is due to their high physical and chemical stability and their efficient emissions within the bio-window band. Nevertheless, further enhancements are needed to optimize the luminescence of MAl2O4-based phosphors for optimal performance. Recently, (YGO):Eu3+@AVCDs (5 wt %) nanocomposites and CDs (7 wt %)@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ nanocomposites have shown a 4-fold and 66.23 fold increase in PL intensity compared to samples without encapsulated CDs [20,21]. An astonishing increase by a factor of 342.82 was noted in the PL intensity upon embedding nitrogen-doped CDs in Ce3+ doped Y2TiWO8 nanophosphor [22]. Additionally, the emission intensity of CDs@MgWO4:Eu3+ and CDs@MgWO4:Tb3+ composites is respectively 5 and 7 times stronger than that of MgWO4:Eu3+/Tb3+ phosphors [23]. The absorption spectra of LaPO4:Eu3+ ions fall within a wavelength band of 260–500 nm, while the emission spectrum of the CQDs ranges from 380 to 550 nm. This results in an overlap between the absorption spectrum of the acceptor and the emission spectrum of the donor. Thus, it can be concluded that there is FRET between Eu3+ and CQDs ions, resulting in an increase in the emission of red-coloured light in the CQD@LaPO4:Eu3+ nanocomposite [24]. Additionally, ZAO:Eu3+ phosphor exhibits high specific luminescence characteristics, surface area, and excellent red emission upon the addition of CDs as hard templates. The multi-emissive phosphorescence observed in CDs@ZAO was attributed to several factors: the protection of CD triplet emission by the ZAO matrix through the confinement effect, the spin-orbit coupling mechanism, and the efficient energy transfer from inorganic trap states to CD triplet states [25].

In forensic investigations, FPs serve as unique patterns that can identify individuals. These patterns are formed at birth and remain unchanged throughout life. When fingers come into contact with an object, their secretions leave FPs impressions that may be barely visible or completely invisible. To render LFPs visible, various visualization methods have been developed. Nevertheless, these methods come with limitations such as complexity, cost, toxicity, low contrast, low sensitivity, and destructiveness. Hence, there is a demand for visualization methods that are straightforward, instantaneous, precise, user-friendly, cost-effective, and non-destructive. One promising approach is the application of luminescent materials to dust LFPs, which can significantly improve the contrast and sensitivity of latent prints [26,27].

In addition to LFPs, counterfeiting and information security pose significant challenges in forensics. These issues revolve around the replication of valuable and essential items, including currency, certificates, branded products, medicines, and food. These illicit practices have severe adverse effects on human health, business integrity, and global social advancement [28]. To combat counterfeiting effectively, AC techniques must be challenging to replicate yet easy to authenticate. Various methods have been proposed to address counterfeiting, including spectroscopic analysis, watermarks, holograms, and metal threads. Indeed, these conventional methods are not fool proof and are susceptible to exploitation by counterfeiters [29,30]. Hence, there is a pressing need for innovative techniques to prevent and detect counterfeiting. One promising avenue is the utilization of luminescent nanomaterials, which generate hidden marks that can be unveiled under specific conditions, akin to LFPs. While the properties of CDs@ZAO have been documented, the impact of CD integration on the luminescence, FPs detection, cheiloscopy, thermal sensing, and AC capabilities of ZAO:Eu3+ remains unexplored. Hence, in this study, CDs were introduced into ZAO:Eu3+ to enhance the PL intensity of the phosphors. The crystallinity, surface morphology, PL properties, and thermal stability of ZAO:Eu3+ phosphors with and without CD incorporation were examined. Furthermore, the optical thermometry characteristics of the CDs@ZAO:Eu3+ phosphor are discussed.

Raw materials included Zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2.6H2O) (98 %), Aluminum nitrate non-ahydrate (Al(NO3)3.9H2O) (98.0 %), Europium(III) nitrate pentahydrate (Eu(NO₃)₃.5H2O) (99.9 %), citric acid (HOC(COOH)(CH2COOH)2) 99.0 % and urea (NH2CONH2) 99.5% are procured from Sigma Aldrich, India. Distilled water is made in the laboratory.

CDs are synthesized via the hydrothermal method, wherein a solution comprising 2 g of citric acid and 1 gm of urea dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water is prepared in a beaker. After stirring the solution on a magnetic stirrer for 10 min at room temperature, it is transferred into a 100 mL stainless steel reactor with a Teflon liner for a hydrothermal reaction at 200 °C for 8 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution is filtered through a 0.22 μm filter to remove larger particles. Following this, the solution is transferred into a 20 mL dialysis kit with a 1 kDa molecular weight cut-off and underwent dialysis in 1.5 L of distilled water 2–3 times for 48 h. This process yielded a pale-yellow solution of CDs. The synthesis mechanism of CDs utilizing urea and citric acid is illustrated in Fig. 1 a.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of ZAO: Eu3+ (1–11 mol %) nanophosphor
ce:section.ce:para: Stoichiometric quantities of Zn(NO3)2, Al(NO3)3 are used as host precursors (oxidizer), Eu(NO₃)₃ as a dopant precursor used without further purification. EGCG (5 mL) is used as bio-surfactant and homogeneously mixed using magnetic stirrer. Further, the resultant mixture is subjected to ultrasonication using probe-sonicator (power: 50 W, intensity: 17.6 Wcm−2, fixed temperature: 75 °C) for 5 h. The solution was kept undisturbed until a white precipitate was formed. Further, the precipitate was filtered and washed thoroughly by using distilled water and ethanol. The obtained product was dried at 60 °C for 3hrs in a vacuum oven followed by calcination at 600 °C for 3hrs. Fig. 1b shows the schematic representation for the synthesis of Eu3+ doped ZnAl2O4 NPs.

To form a composite, prepared optimized ZAO:7Eu3+ phosphor was taken in a China clay bowl. CDs prepared using the carbonization method was added and this mixture was homogenized using mortar and pestle. Now the bowl was kept in an oven at 80 °C for 12 h. The prepared composite was allowed to cool to room temperature. The prepared composite was used for further characterization. The synthesis of CDs and the fabrication process of CDs@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs are depicted schematically in Fig. 1c.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Latent fingerprints and lip print development
ce:section.ce:para: A 25-year-old female volunteered her FPs and LPs. Prior to imprinting on various substrates to create FP, both her hands and lips are meticulously cleansed with deionized water. Subsequently, she touched her fingers and lips to a variety of substrates to produce the FPs and LPs. Next, the LFPs and LPs are delicately dusted with the CDs(5 wt%)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs using a FPs brush. After excess powder is removed using an air blower, photographs of the FPs and lip print (LP), visualized by the phosphors under both normal light and ultraviolet (UV) 365 nm light, are captured using a digital camera (Nikon D3100/AF-S digital camera with 12 megapixels).

A solution is created by blending distilled water (83 %), NH4OH (1 % w/w) to regulate the pH, and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) acting as a binder. Sodium dodecyl sulfonate at a concentration of 3 mg/L is incorporated into the mixture to adjust the surface tension of the inks. The mixture underwent mechanical stirring for 45 min to ensure complete dissolution. Following this, 0.5 g of CDs(5 wt%)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs is introduced into the solution, which is then stirred continuously for 30 min and subjected to ultrasonic treatment at 25 kHz for 60 min to achieve a uniform dispersion. The formulated security ink is applied to create AC patterns on a range of substrates (plastic, painted wood, currency, paper, aluminum foil, glass and metal sheet) using a simple sketch pen and brush technique. The resulting patterns are photographed under both daylight and UV light at 365 nm illumination.

The phase purity of the obtained material is assessed using a Shimadzu X-ray diffractometer (PXRD-7000) equipped with CuKα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å). Furthermore, Rietveld refining of the XRD patterns is conducted using the GSAS-2 software. Microstructural and elemental compositional analysis are conducted using a field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) and a transmission electron microscope (TEM; JEM-2010) to study particle size. Furthermore, the binding energies (BE) of the constituent elements are analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) with an ESCALABMKII spectrometer. The spectrofluorometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon Fluorolog-3) used for PL measurements is equipped with a 450-Watt Xenon lamp as the excitation source.

Fig. 2 a illustrates the XRD patterns of the ZAO:Eu3+ (1–11 mol %) NPs, CDs, and CDs (1, 3, 5 & 7 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs. The diffraction peaks align precisely with the cubic phase of ZAO, characterized by a Fd-3m (227) space group according to the standard JCPDS Card No. 05–0669 [31], indicating the absence of impurities and successful incorporation of Eu3+ ions into the ZAO lattice. The XRD pattern of the CDs displays broad peaks centered at 2θ = 20° and 40°, corresponding to the (002) and (100) planes of amorphous carbon. Additionally, the characteristic diffraction peaks of ZAO:7Eu3+ and CDs are simultaneously observed in the ZAO:Eu3+ sample [32]. Fig. 2a1 depicts the enlarged XRD patterns within the 2θ range of 35.5–37.5°. The gradual shift of the domain peaks of the ZAO:Eu3+ towards the lower angle side can be attributed to Bragg's law [33]. This phenomenon occurs because the larger Eu3+ ion (r = 0.947 Å) replaces the smaller Zn2+ ion (r = 0.74 Å) in the matrix, leading to an enlargement of the interplanar spacing and a decrease in the diffraction peak angle θ. Additionally, to confirm the substitution of Eu3+ ions in the ZAO matrix, the percentage difference of the radii (Dr) is calculated using the following equation [34]:

In the equation, Rs represents the radius of the replaced ions (Zn2+ or Al3+), Rd represents the radius of the doped Eu3+ ions, and CN represents the coordination number. The Eu3+ ion, with a coordination number (CN) of 6, has an ionic radius of 0.947 Å, while Zn2+ (CN = 6) has an ionic radius of 0.74 Å, and Al3+ (CN = 6) has an ionic radius of 0.535 Å. As a result, the percentage difference (Dr) between the ionic radii of Zn2+ (CN = 6) and Eu3+ (CN = 6) is calculated to be 27.9 %, which is below the threshold of 30 %. This indicates that Eu3+ can effectively substitute for Zn2+ ions. However, for the Al3+ ion (Dr = 77 %), it does not meet the criteria for substitution [35]. The substitution of Zn2+ by Eu3+ requires charge compensation, which is achieved by introducing defects with positive or negative charges, resulting in defect-association interactions. comprises three defects. Crystalline analysis revealed a minor shift of the XRD peaks towards lower diffraction angles following the substitution of Eu3+ for Zn2+ (Fig. 2a1). The larger size of the Eu3+ ion replacing the relatively smaller Zn2+ leads to an increase in the interplanar spacing (d). The average crystallite sizes of ZAO:Eu3+ NPs are determined using the Scherrer's method, with the obtained values presented in Table S1. The data shows a decrease in crystallite size with increasing Eu3+ concentration. Additionally, the crystallite size is estimated using various methods, and the corresponding plots are provided in Figs. S1 and S2.

Rietveld refinement, facilitated by GSAS software [37], is employed to refine the phase structure of both undoped and ZAO:7Eu3+ NPs (Fig. 2b and c). The Rietveld refinement patterns of ZAO:Eu3+ (1, 3, 5, 9, 11) are depicted in Fig. S3, with relevant data presented in Table S2. The Bragg parameters listed in Table S2 are observed within 10%, indicating a successful refinement process. The pseudo-Voigt function effectively refined the profiles' shapes. Furthermore, Vesta 3.5.8 software [38] is utilized to process the refinement data into a CIF file format, enabling the acquisition of the three-dimensional (3D) crystal structure of ZAO, along with the coordination environment of the cationic sites within the unit cell, as illustrated in Fig. 2d. Fig. 2e illustrates the coordination environment of [AlO6] and [ZnO4].

FE-SEM technique was used to understand the morphology of ZAO:7Eu3+ (1–11mol %) NPs, CDs and CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ composite (Fig. 3 a–h). The FE-SEM images of ZAO:7Eu3+ (1–11mol %) NPs reveal agglomeration-free hexagonal-shaped particles across all samples (Fig. 3a–f). Fig. 3g displays the FE-SEM image of CDs distinctly exhibit spherical particles with well-defined edges. Fig. 3h shows the FE-SEM image of CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ composite clearly showed the CDs are embedded on the phosphor. From the using particle size distribution histograms, the average particle size was estimated and shown in Fig. 3i–k. As can be seen from the histograms, the estimated particle size is found to be ∼0.148 μm (ZAO:7Eu3+) and ∼5.28 nm (CDs) respectively. However, in the case of CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ composite the average particle size for phosphor and CDs is found to be ∼0.80–0.150 μm and ∼5.29 nm respectively.

The microscopic properties of the CDs were determined using TEM analysis and are shown in Fig. 4 a. The results indicate that the particles are spherical, homogeneously monodispersed, and with no form of aggregation. As can be seen from the image, the particle size is found to be 4–6 nm. In addition, the average particle size is estimated using ImageJ software and the corresponding distribution histogram is depicted in Fig. 4b. The estimated size is found to be ∼5.2 nm. The HR-TEM image (Fig. 4c) showed distinct lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of 0.34 nm corresponding to the (002) of the lattice planes of CDs and implies high crystallinity of the CDs. Fig. 4d shows the TEM image of ZAO:7Eu3+ NPs clearly reveals individual particles with size ∼20–50 nm. In addition, the particle size distribution histogram is used to estimate the average particle size and is found to be ∼32.5 nm (Fig. 4e). Further, the HR-TEM image of ZAO:7Eu3+ NPs is depicted in Fig. 4f and the inter-planar distance (d) of the phosphor ∼0.24 nm, which is located in the (311) plane of ZAO. The TEM image of CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ composite is shown in Fig. 4g and the corresponding average particle size of CDs in composite is found to be 5.22 nm (Fig. 4h). HR-TEM image of CDs@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs is shown in Fig. 4i. The estimated d spacing value of (002), (311) and (220) planes are found to be 0.34, 0.24 and 0.28 nm respectively. In addition, the d spacing is also calculated using GATAN software [39], revealed values of 0.34, 0.24 and 0.28 nm for (002), (311), and (220) planes, respectively (Fig. 4j-l). Significantly, the inter-planar d-spacing obtained from HR-TEM images closely matches with the results obtained from GATAN software.

XPS was further utilized to confirm related surface element compositions and chemical bonding states after different coating modifications. Fig. 5 (a-n) present the XPS spectra for ZAO, ZAO:7Eu3+ and CDs (5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs, as well as deconvoluted high-resolution core spectra. Fig. 5a confirms the doping of Eu in ZAO NPs and grafted Carbon in the ZAO:7Eu3+ NPs. The Zn 2p spectra in ZAO, ZAO:7Eu3+ and CDs (5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs exhibit two prominent peaks Fig. 5(b–e & i), corresponding to the Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 binding energies, respectively. These peaks at 1021.6 and 1044.6 eV in Fig. 5b are attributed to Zn2+ ions in tetrahedral positions, with an energy difference of 23.1 eV. These results support that the Zn ions exist in 2+ oxidation state. The Al 2p spectra exhibit a well-defined Gaussian peak centered at 73.5 eV, 73.6 eV and 73.7 eV in ZAO, ZAO:7Eu3+ and CDs (5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs respectively [40], suggesting that the majority of Al3+ ions occupy octahedral positions, as depicted in Fig. 5(c–f &j). In the fitted XPS spectrum of the O 1s core spectra (Fig. 5d–g & k), the peak with binding energy at 530.3, 530.4 and 530.7 eV, is associated with Zn–O bonds in tetrahedral sites of ZAO, ZAO:7Eu3+ and CDs (5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs respectively [41]. High-resolution XPS spectra of ZAO:7Eu3+ samples in the energy region of the Eu-3d transition are presented in Fig. 5h, displaying four peaks attributed to the spin-orbit splitting of the 3d state into 3d5/2 (1142.8 eV and 1135 eV) and 3d3/2 (1164.7 eV and 1154.5 eV) of Eu3+ ions [42]. The Eu 3d deconvoluted spectrum of CDs (5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs indicates similar results with a small variation in binding energy. The deconvolution of the high-resolution C 1s spectrum of synthesized NPs illustrates three discernible peaks at 284.8 eV, 287.1 eV, and 289 eV, representing sp3 bulk-bonded carbon C–C, C–O chemical bonds, and CO bonds, respectively (Fig. 5m). The peak observed at approximately 284.8 eV corresponds to C–C sp3 hybridization, potentially arising from sp3 C–H bonding or a diamond-like structure. The peak at 287.1 eV may be attributed to C–O–C or C–O–H bonds, while an additional sub-peak at around 289 eV is assigned to C(O)OH or HO–COH bonds [43]. The high-resolution N1s XPS peak of CDs (5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs appeared at 400 eV, which contributed from the pyrrolic N depicted in (Fig. 5n). The spectrum can be fitted with 2 Gaussian peaks at 399.6 and 400.4 eV that correspond to the pyrrolic N and graphite N, respectively [44]. Existence of Pyrrolic N indicates that N bonds with C that are formed from de-hydrolysis between the neighbour carboxyl and amide groups. Graphite N originates from the N atoms that are bonded with 3 neighbouring C atoms.

Fig. 6 a displays the excitation spectrum of ZAO:7Eu3+ with a monitored wavelength of 618 nm. This spectrum is characterized by two distinct regions: a broad band spanning from 225 to 350 nm and narrow excitation peaks ranging from 350 to 475 nm. The broad band around 269 nm is attributed to the charge transfer band (CTB) from O2− to Eu3+. Other peak centers are observed at 361 nm (7F0→5D4), 380 nm (7F0→5G3), 393 nm (7F0→5L6), 415 nm (7F0→5D3), and 464 nm (7F0→5D2) [45]. These peaks originate from the 4f-4f transition within the Eu3+ ion configuration. It's noteworthy that the optimal excitation wavelength identified from the excitation spectrum is 393 nm, which aligns well with n-UV chips. Fig. 6b illustrates the PL spectra of ZAO:Eu3+ phosphor under 393 nm excitation. The PL spectra predominantly feature four intense and sharp peaks corresponding to the 539 nm (5D0→7F5), 595 nm (5D0→7F1), 618 nm (5D0→7F2), 654 nm (5D0→7F3), and 706 nm (5D0→7F4) transitions of Eu3+ [46]. The orange emission peak at 595 nm (5D0→7F1) is associated with a magnetic dipole (MD) transition, whereas the red peaks at 618 nm originate from the electric dipole (ED) transition of (5D0→7F2). As per the Judd-Ofelt theory, the ED electric dipole transition is more responsive to the local symmetry of Eu3+ in the crystal compared to the MD transition. The asymmetric ratio (R/O), derived from the integral IED/IMD, serves as a metric for analyzing the crystal field environment surrounding Eu3+ ions. The R/O value exceeding unity suggests that Eu3+ ions reside in a site lacking inversion symmetry, where the electric dipole (ED) transition (red) predominates. In contrast, when the chemical environment exhibits inversion symmetry, the magnetic dipole (MD) transition (orange-red) becomes prominent. In the case of the ZAO:Eu3+ phosphor, the calculated IED/IMD value of 3.27 underscores the prevalence of the electric dipole (ED) transition [47]. The detailed concentration quenching mechanism is discussed in supplementary section (Figs. S5a and b)

Fig. 7 a demonstrates the strong excitation capability of the CDs material, particularly at longer UV wavelengths around 358 nm. The maximum absorption peak observed at 358 nm in the ultraviolet spectrum suggests a singular surface state for the carbon quantum dots, driven by the CDs n-π* transition of the CO band and the π-π* transition of the conjugated CC band. The research posits two primary explanations for the PL behaviour of CDs: size variation and distribution of surface energy traps. These phenomena combine to induce fluorescence in CDs, involving emission from conjugated π domain band gap transitions and surface defects. The surface functionalization motifs, such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups containing oxygen, enhance CDs solubility in aqueous solutions, offering advantages over graphene quantum dots, which are typically poorly soluble in common solvents [48]. Consequently, CDs exhibit superior PL excitation properties.

Fig. 7b shows the excitation spectra for CDs@ZAO:7Eu3+ and ZAO:7Eu3+ phosphors. When monitored at 618 nm, the PLE spectrum of ZAO:7Eu3+ exhibits numerous sharp peaks, characteristic of the typical intra-configurational f-f transitions of Eu3+ ions. In comparison to ZAO:7Eu3+, the excitation spectrum of CDs@ZAO:7Eu3+ features a prominent broad and strong band between 200 and 400 nm, in addition to the absorption peak corresponding to Eu3+. This broad band is attributed to the π-π* and n-π* transitions of CDs [49,50]. The results strongly demonstrate the energy transfer from CDs to Eu3+ in CDs@ZAO:7Eu3+ nanocomposites. Additionally, these nanocomposites exhibit a stronger excitation peak compared to the bare ZAO:7Eu3+ NPs. Moreover, a near-UV wavelength of 395 nm can simultaneously excite both CDs and ZAO:7Eu3+ NPs.

Fig. 7c presents the PL spectra of CDs@ZAO:7Eu3+ nanocomposites and ZAO:7Eu3+ NPs under 393 nm excitation. The broad emission band from 250 to 550 nm corresponds to the emission of CDs. Additionally, the PL spectra of both CDs@ZAO:7Eu3+ and ZAO:7Eu3+ samples predominantly display four intense and sharp peaks, particularly at 539 nm (5D0→7F5), 595 nm (5D0→7F1), 618 nm (5D0→7F2), 654 nm (5D0→7F3), and 706 nm (5D0→7F4) transitions of Eu3+. Furthermore, the luminescence intensity of CDs@ZAO:7Eu3+ nanocomposites is significantly enhanced due to the energy transfer from CDs to Eu3+ ions. To confirm this energy transfer, we measured the PL spectrum of CDs and the PLE spectrum of ZAO:7Eu3+. As shown in Fig. 7d, the absorption peaks of Eu3+ ions at 361, 380, 393, 415, and 465 nm overlap with the strong emission band of CDs ranging from 350 to 550 nm. This overlap further confirms the energy transfer from CDs to Eu3+ ions [51].

Fig. 7e clearly illustrates the luminescence and energy transfer processes between CDs and ZAO:7Eu3+. Initially, the CDs absorb UV light, causing electrons to transfer from the C π to the C π* energy level. The electrons then relax to the lowest LUMO energy level through C π*-N π* and N π*-O π* processes. As the electrons transition back to the C π energy level, blue emission occurs, and energy is transferred to the Eu3+ ions. Concurrently, the Eu3+ ions absorb this photon energy, resulting in the transition from the 7F0 to the 5D4 energy level. Through non-radiative transitions, the electrons relax to the 5D0 energy level. They then transition to the 7F0 (539 nm), 7F1 (595 nm), 7F2 (618 nm), 7F3 (654 nm), and 7F4 (706 nm) energy levels, respectively. Moreover, the excited electrons at the 5D1-4 levels of Eu3+ are captured by CDs and subsequently redirected to the 5D0 energy level through a FRET process in CDs@ZAO:7Eu3+nanocomposites. This leads to an increase in the number of electrons at the 5D0 level of Eu3+. Consequently, it is inferred that the energy loss associated with the non-radiative transition of Eu3+ (5D4→5D0) is significantly reduced through the capability of CDs to capture electrons (EC). Simultaneously, the energy transfer (ET) between CDs and Eu3+ enhances the emission intensities at 539, 595, 616, 654 and 706 nm.

The CIE coordinates (x, y) for ZAO:Eu3+ (1–11 mol %) NPs and CDs(1, 3, 5, 7 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs are determined based on their PL spectra under 393 nm excitation, as summarized in Table 1 . Fig. 7f illustrates the CIE diagram of ZAO:Eu3+ (1–11 mol %) NPs and CDs(1, 3, 5, 7 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs. Notably, CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs exhibited vivid red emission under a 365 nm UV lamp. The CIE coordinates (0.6140, 0.3828) of the CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs are positioned within the bright red region, closely resembling the ideal red light coordinates of (0.670, 0.330) [51]. CP values for both NPs and NCs are determined using the following equation [52]:

For the optimal NCs in this study, with (x, y) = (0.6140, 0.3828), the calculated CP value is 99.5%, exceeding that of reported Eu3+ doped red phosphors such as Na2Gd(PO4)(MoO4):Eu3+ (92%) and Ca2SiO4: Eu3+ (91.2%). The CCT diagram is show in Fig. 7g and the estimated CIE coordinates, CP and Correlated colour temperature (CCT) values of ZAO:Eu3+ (1–11 mol %) NPs and CDs(1, 3, 5, 7 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs are listed in Table 1, all of which exceed 98 %. Consequently, the CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs are characterized as high CP red-emitting phosphors. Where (xi, yi) denote the specific CIE coordinates for the analyzed phosphor, with the corresponding CCT values displayed in Table 1. With the increase in the concentration of Eu3+ and CDs, the CCT decreased from 1375 to 1314 K, resulting in a transition of the light profile from neutral white emission to warm white emission. Warm white light is known to alleviate eye fatigue. Typically, phosphors with a CCT value of >5000 K are suited for cold white light applications, while those with a CCT value of <5000 K are suitable for warm white light [54]. The NCs under investigation is well-suited for warm white light applications. Consequently, Eu3+ doped phosphors hold significant value for indoor lighting applications [55].

Verification of energy transfer from CDs to ZAO:7Eu3+ in CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs is accomplished by comparing the luminescence decay curves observed at 437 nm (CDs luminescence) of CDs before and after their incorporation into the ZAO:7Eu3+ NPs excited at 358 nm. Fig. 7h–j presents the luminescent decay curves illustrating the transfer of energy from CDs to ZAO:7Eu3+ in CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs. These decay curves can be effectively fitted using single or double exponential functions [56]:

The decay curves of CDs luminescence for ZAO:7Eu3+ NPs and CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs are well fitted to double exponential function rather than a single exponential function, as depicted in Fig. 7i and j. This double exponential decay behaviour is commonly observed in luminescent materials experiencing energy transfer phenomena. Utilizing equations (12) and (13), the average lifetimes of ZAO:7Eu3+ and CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs are approximately 2.18 and 1.75 ns, respectively, which is lower than that of CDs alone. This disparity implies the occurrence of energy transfer between CDs and ZAO:Eu3+ NPs through the FRET mechanism.

The IQE of the phosphor plays a crucial role in assessing its suitability for application in white LEDs (w-LEDs). Fig. 7k illustrates a comparison between the excitation line of the BaSO4 reference and the emission spectra of ZAO:7Eu3+ NPs and CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs. The IQE value can be calculated using below Eqn [58].:

Where LS represents the emission spectrum of ZAO:7Eu3+ NPs and CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs, while ER and ES denote the excitation spectra of the BaSO4 reference material and ZAO:7Eu3+ NPs and CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs, respectively. When excited at a wavelength of 393 nm, the IQE values of the NPs and NCs reach 62.65 % and 87.01 %, respectively, surpassing those reported in the literature as listed in Table S4 [59–66] and Fig. S7.

To evaluate the practical applicability of CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs in real-world w-LEDs, stability is paramount. Hence, a comprehensive examination of the optical characteristics of CDs(5 wt %) and CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs under various conditions including air, heat, and UV irradiation is conducted. To address potential fluorescence quenching caused by aggregation, the stability of CDs is evaluated by dispersing them in an aqueous solution. Initially, the samples are continuously exposed to UV irradiation using a portable UV lamp to gauge their resistance. Fig. S8 depicts the PL spectra of UV- and thermally-treated CDs(5 wt %) and CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs. The variation in PL intensity of CDs and CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs under different UV exposure durations (0–250 min) is illustrated in Fig. 8 a. The photo-bleaching process results in a fluorescence intensity reduction of CDs to 58 % [67]. Conversely, the NCs exhibit enhanced resilience to UV radiation, retaining 93 % of the initial PL intensity even after 250 min of exposure. The 7 % decrease in fluorescence intensity is attributed to photo-oxidation [68,69]. This mechanism entails the formation of superoxide species on the surface upon UV exposure, followed by rapid surface oxidation, the generation of water, gradual hydration of the internal ZAO:7Eu3+, and eventual degradation of the material lattice [70]. To assess thermal stability, CDs and CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs are treated to 100 °C for 10 min and then cooled naturally at room temperature. PL intensity measurements are taken after each cycle, as shown in Fig. 8b. After 7 heating-cooling cycles, the PL intensity of CDs decreased by 11%, while that of CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ decreased by only 5 %, indicating superior thermal durability of the composites. Lastly, PL intensity is monitored every 3 days over a period of 15 days while maintaining CDs and NCs in an air atmosphere (20–24 °C and 50–80 % humidity). While the PL intensity of CDs showed degradation over time, that of the composites remained largely stable (Fig. 8c and d). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the composites on both day 1 and day 15 (Fig. S9) remained almost identical, suggesting an unchanged crystal structure of the composites in air, which aligns with the consistent PL intensity. This research affirms the exceptional resilience of CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ composites to UV exposure, heat, and air, credited to the safeguarding influence of ZAO:7Eu3+ [71]. This robust stability highlights the promising potential of CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ composites in w-LEDs.

The thermal stability of a material is crucial in determining its suitability for applications in solid-state lighting. To evaluate the thermal endurance of both ZAO:7Eu3+ and CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs, Temperature dependent photoluminescence (TDPL) spectra are analyzed across temperatures ranging from 300 to 480 K, employing a 393 nm excitation wavelength. Fig. 9 a–d illustrate the 3D colour contour representing the temperature and TDPL spectra of ZAO:7Eu3+ NPs and CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs. Notably, the location and configuration of the emission peak remain consistent as the temperature rises. As shown in Fig. 9e, at a temperature of 420 K, the residual luminescence intensity of ZAO:7Eu3+ and CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs are 87.70 % and 90.06 %, respectively, significantly higher than that of Sr2Si5N8:Eu2+ (81.98 %). Furthermore, these values surpass those of many reported phosphors. Table S5 provides a comparison among different phosphors [72–78], highlighting the outstanding thermal stability of CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs.

The process of thermal quenching in emission can be explained through a configurational coordinate diagram (CCD), as depicted in Fig. 9f. In this diagram, the Ue parabola represents the Crystal Field Stark Splitting (CFSS) formed by the overlap of the Zn–O and Eu−O. The 5Dj and 7Fj states correspond to the excited and ground states of the Eu3+ ions, respectively, and are contingent upon a generalized configuration coordinate denoted by R. Within the parabolas, horizontal lines represent the energy levels of the vibrational states. The intersection points of the 5Dj and Ue parabola, referred to as crossover point A, mark the juncture where the energy of the vibrational states of the 5Dj and Ue electronic states converges. At this crossover point A, the thermal activation energy, symbolized by ΔE, denotes the energy differential between point A and the bottom of the 5Dj electronic state. At the outset, electrons in the 7Fj state absorb photon energy, transitioning to the excited state (5Dj) via path (1) of photoexcitation, thus disrupting the equilibrium of the system. Subsequently, electrons proceed along a non-radiative path to reach the bottom of the 5Dj state. At room temperature, electrons revert to the 7Fj state through path (3) of radiative relaxation. The emitted photon energy typically decreases compared to the excitation energy, owing to energy loss during lattice relaxation to achieve the excited steady state. However, with rising temperature, lattice vibrations become more prominent, resulting in a higher count of thermally active phonons. These phonons interact with the excited electrons at the bottom of the 5Dj state, matching their energy with that of the crossover point A. When they reach point A (path 2), electrons dissipate their energy non-radiatively through path (4) of phonon relaxation. With increasing temperature, the interaction between electrons and phonons strengthens, leading to more electrons relaxing non-radiatively to the ground state [79].

The plot of ln [(I0/I)−1] versus 1/T for ZAO:7Eu3+ and CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs is depicted in Fig. 9g and h. The activation energy (Ea) can be determined using equation (16), providing insights into the thermal quenching mechanism of ZAO:7Eu3+ and CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs [80]:
(13)

)
In this equation, Ea symbolizes the thermal activation energy, T represents the temperature, I0 stands for the luminescence intensity at a temperature close to zero, I(T) denotes the emission intensity at various temperatures T, c is a constant, and k is the Boltzmann coefficient, which has a fixed value of 8.62 × 10−5 eV/k. The calculated values of Ea are determined as 0.23 eV for ZAO:7Eu3+ and 0.27 eV for CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs. These values exceed those reported for other phosphors (Table 2 ) [81–89]. The higher Ea indicates that electrons require high energy to overcome the barrier, resulting in better thermal stability. Consequently, CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs demonstrate excellent thermal stability.

Fig. 9b–d, and e illustrate the TDPL spectra and the normalized intensity of ZAO:7Eu3+ and CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs, focusing on the intense peak at 618 nm, as a function of temperature. It is observed that as the temperature increases, the intensity of the peak diminishes. The non-uniformity in the rate of decrease is utilized for Fluorescence Intensity Ratio (FIR)-based luminescence thermometry. The temperature dependence for I595/I618 in ZAO:7Eu3+ and CDs(5 wt%)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs is illustrated in Fig. 9k & l, respectively. The maximum Sr at 300 K for ZAO:7Eu3+ and CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs is 0.55 % K−1 and 1.36 % K−1, respectively. The Sr value of CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs is high compared to reported phosphors can be seen in Table 3 [19,20,22,92–99]. This high SR value indicates the phosphors excellent signal-sensing capability.

Fig. S10 shows the variance of the x and y coordinates of ZAO:7Eu3+ and CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs from 300 K to 480 K. Upon calculation, the variances Sx 2 and Sy 2 of the CIE coordinates are determined to be 1.5 × 10−5 and 2.3 × 10−5 (ZAO:7Eu3+) and 1.3 × 10−5 and 1.7 × 10−5 (CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs), respectively. These values serve as direct evidence of the remarkable thermal stability of the NCs. These findings underscore the suitability of CDs(5 wt%)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs as an excellent material for application in w-LEDs. represent the chromaticity coordinates in the uniform color space u' v', while x and y denote the CIE coordinates within the chromaticity diagram (CIE 1931). Where 0 and t denote the chromaticity coordinates at temperatures of 300 K and 480 K, respectively. The trend of chromaticity coordinate changes with temperature for CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs under 365 nm excitation is depicted in Fig. S10. Notably, the chromaticity coordinates for CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs are (0.6333, 0.3594) at 300 K and (0.6151, 0.3751) at 480 K, resulting in a calculated ΔS at 480 K of 0.0356. Such a minimal shift suggests that the CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs, CDs, and ZAO:7Eu3+ NPs demonstrate the capability to sustain colour consistency even when subjected to higher temperatures.

For comparison purposes, LFPs developed using ZAO:7Eu3+ NPs and CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs are depicted in Fig. 10 a and b. The FPs ridge width observed with CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs measures 240 μm, which is closer to the mean ridge width (280 μm) compared to ZAO:7Eu3+ NPs (197 μm). This suggests that ZAO:7Eu3+ NPs are more prone to aggregation than CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs. At the micro level, FPs features are categorized into three levels based on pattern characteristics. Level I feature include overarching patterns, enabling classification into three typical types: loop, arch, and whorl. Level II features encompass partial FPs minutiae, including ridge termination, ridge bifurcation, island, spur, and others. These Level I and II features serve as primary pattern data for automatic FPs identification technology. Level III features constitute critical pattern data, offering greater precision and serving for finer comparative matches, including sweat pores, creases and scars. These details enhance the accuracy of FPs identification and comparison processes. While both powders enable clear visibility of level I and level II features, the CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs exhibit finer and more precise level III features compared to ZAO:7Eu3+ NPs. The 3D interactive profiles of FPs drawn using ImageJ software, as shown in Fig. 10(a1 & b1), illustrate the uniform distribution of CDs(5 wt%)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs on FPs compared to ZAO:7Eu3+ NPs [100]. Additionally, observation from the images reveals that both phosphor and composites are exclusively attached to the ridges, while the grooves remain empty. This is corroborated by the pixel profile data obtained for the fluorescent images of the three systems, as demonstrated in Fig. 10(a11 & b11). The highlighted area in the respective photographs indicates the region selected for pixel profile analysis. Fluctuations in pixel values align with the profile of ridges and grooves/furrows, with maximum and minimum pixel values corresponding to ridges and grooves, respectively. This confirms the excellent selectivity of the phosphor powder over the ridges and furrows of the FPs, essential for LFPs detection.
Note: FPs development and visualization on various substrates, FP features analysis using SEM, FPs treated under various experimental conditions are shown in Figs. S11–S13.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Poroscopy studies on male and female FPs
ce:section.ce:para: To determine sex using FP, thumb impressions from male and female donors of the same age (25 years) are collected and visualized using CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs. The FPs are analyzed for level I, II, and III features (Fig. 11 ). In the male FPs analysis (Fig. 11a), patterns such as the right loop (1) and ridges constituted the first-level details, while features like islands (2), ridge ends (3), hooks (5), enclosures (6), and bifurcations (7) are considered level II details or FPs minutiae. In the female FPs analysis (Fig. 11f), similar patterns are observed at the level I, with additional features such as short ridges (4) and dots (7) identified as level II details or FPs minutiae. Level III details included pores, ridge spaces, scars and deltas. The level II and III details are crucial for individualization. Utilizing fluorescence emission with CDs(5 wt%)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs, islands observed in male FPs appear longer compared to those in female FP. Angular ridge ends are nearly identical between males and females, while features like hooks are observed in both sexes. Third-level details, also known as poroscopy, include features such as pores, which are particularly significant in forensic investigations but can be challenging to develop and analyse comprehensively using conventional powders under various conditions. These pores are believed to be formed by sweat pores.

These distinctive characteristics often serve as confirmatory signatures for individual identification. The poroscopic variances between males and females are illustrated in Table S7. Poroscopic details such as (a) position, (b) diameter, (c) separation, (d) area and (e) shape are depicted in Fig. 11(a8 & f8). Positions of pores, such as C (centre) and (OS) one side of the ridge, are observed in both male and female FP. Females tend to have smaller diameters and areas in their FPs compared to males. However, no significant differences are observed in pore separation between male and female FP. Male FPs exhibit circle, square, triangle, and oval-shaped pores, whereas female FPs predominantly feature triangle, square, and circular pores.

Significant sex-related differences in thumbprint ridge count are observed in the upper right (UR) and upper left (UL) areas analyzed in this study. Instead of restricting the count solely to the region between the core and delta, we opted to tally the ridges across the entire fingertip. Since the delta is absent in female FP, an imaginary line is drawn from the core to the end of the FPs on both the left (UL) and right (UR) sides of the core. The core point, which marks the turning point of the innermost ridge, is crucial in biometrics and FPs scanning for various purposes such as indexing, classification, alignment, orientation field modelling, and identification or verification. This region is characterized by higher-than-normal ridge curvature and consistent ridge flow in both directions. For the study of ridge count, the angle between them is standardized at 90 ⁰. Dots are disregarded in the count, while forks are assessed as two ridges excluding the handle, and a lake is similarly counted as two ridges. Let 'a' represent the ridge count (R.C) on the upper left (UL) side (along the yellow line of FPs), and 'b' denote the ridge count on the upper right (UR) side (along the blue line on FPs). The total ridge count is computed as the average of these counts, i.e.,
(17)
R . C =
1 2
(
a + b
)

Ridge counting is conducted within these specified regions, and the results are documented (Table S8). The ridge count of female FPs is observed to be higher than that of male FPs. Additionally, another parameter, the size of the thumb tip area of the FPs, is calculated in square millimetres (mm2) for both male and female FPs using ImageJ software [100]. The area of the female thumb tip FPs is measured to be 429.411 mm2, whereas the male thumbprint area is found to be larger, at 513.595 mm2.

Our results reveal a consistent trend with recent studies examining FPs ridge density and count, indicating that females generally possess a higher FPs ridge count than males. Despite variations in the analyzed areas compared to previous research [101–105], the fundamental quantitative differences persist, reaffirming that females typically exhibit a greater finger (thumb) print ridge count than males, consistent across various ethnic groups. The coarser nature of finger ridges in males compared to females results in a lower ridge count in males within a given area. This phenomenon is largely due to the finer epidermal ridges typically observed in females, with males exhibiting coarser ridges, approximating a difference of around 10 %. Furthermore, we concur with the explanation proposed by Krishna et al. suggesting that the discrepancy in finger ridge count between males and females could be due to the larger average body proportions of males compared to females [106]. Consequently, the same number of ridges may be distributed over a larger surface area in males, resulting in a lower ridge count observed among males.

The 3D interactive plots of the FPs are illustrated in Fig. 11(b and g), demonstrating that males typically possess coarser finger ridges compared to females. It is evident that the number of ridges is higher in Fig. 11g, representing females, than in Fig. 11b, representing males. In Fig. 11 (c & h), the 3D interactive plot of the sweat pore is depicted, with the sweat pores highlighted using white circles (the plotting parameters are provided in Table S9). The luminance of the image is translated into height for the plot. Notably, under 365 nm excitation in the bright field, the CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs exhibit selectivity, adhering primarily to the edges of the sweat pore while barely interacting with the sweat pore itself, resulting in bright white edges and dark sweat pores. To further illustrate the remarkable developing selectivity achieved with our NCs-based FPs development method, we present the 3D interactive plot of a selected pore of a FPs in Fig. 11 (d & i) (the plotting parameters are provided in Table S9). The stark contrast between the peaks and valleys is highly evident, enabling clear differentiation between open and closed pores. Typically, height (depth) information is utilized to analyse the quantitative material deposition on lipid surfaces [107], underscoring the precision of LFPs development using our prepared NCs. Additionally, the grayscale value curve of a selected area of a FPs is depicted in Fig. 11 (e & j), where the distinct contrast between peaks and valleys indicates the continuous variation in grayscale values along the selected area of the latent prints, signifying the presence of grooves and ridges on the FPs. The grayscale change curve exhibited a notable difference between the maximum value (120) and the minimum value (17.5), with a gap of 102.5 arbitrary units (a. u.). The slight fluctuations observed in the plot peaks can be attributed to the presence of sweat pores. Specifically, the gray value difference for a sweat pore is calculated to be 25.67 a. u., confirming the effectiveness of CNs for visualizing latent FPs. Given the simplicity of the entire development process, which requires minimal post-processing, CDs(5 wt%)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs hold promising potential for crime scene investigations.

Identification holds significant importance in crime investigations. While DNA and FPs are well-established methods, they may not always be present at crime scenes. In such scenarios, exploring alternative techniques becomes essential. One emerging avenue is the examination of lip wrinkles. Cheiloscopy, a method based on the unique arrangement of lines on the lips, offers a promising approach to identifying individuals. Given the limited attention given to LP analysis in modern forensic practices, and considering the demand for forensic dental services across various regions, there is a recognized need to explore the use of LPs for identification purposes. As a result, a pilot study is initiated to assess the potential of using NCs for visualizing LPs through a powder dusting method [108].

The categorization system outlined by K. Suzuki and Y. Tsuchihashi [109] is followed rigorously to define the patterns of the grooves, with stringent criteria applied to distinguish Type II, III, and IV patterns. More precisely, Type II patterns are identified by bifurcating lines that did not align in the same straight line as the leg of the 'Y', whereas lines displaying trifurcation and multiple branching are categorized as Type V. Type III patterns comprised lines intersecting to form an ‘X’ pattern, with equal arm lengths from the point of intersection and no superimposition. Groove patterns displaying multiple interconnections and defying categorization into Types I to IV are directly categorized as Type V. The images of upper and lower LPs are divided into four quadrants: Quadrant I for Upper Right, Quadrant II for Upper Left, Quadrant III for Lower Left, and Quadrant IV for Lower Right, as illustrated schematically in Fig. 12 a [110]. LPs from male and female donors of the same age (25 years) are collected for this study. Using CDs(5 wt%)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs, the LPs are developed on aluminum foil and visualized under 365 nm UV light. The resulting images are divided into four quadrants, and the extracted groove patterns from each quadrant are depicted in Fig. 12b and c. The findings revealed that no two LPs exhibited similar characteristics, consistent with previous reports (Fig. 12b1). In female LPs, the prevalence of groove patterns followed the order I1>I > II > III > V > IV. Conversely, in male LPs, Type III is the most prevalent, while Type IV is less prevalent (Fig. 12c1) [111].

Generally, the luminous intensity of phosphors tends to decrease over time, underscoring the importance of monitoring luminous stability as time progresses. The LP images and corresponding red pixel profiles over 1, 90, and 270 days are depicted in Fig. 12(d and e). Minimal visual variation is observed in the LP images over time. In Fig. 12e, after 270 days, the red pixel values exhibit only about a 10% decrease, indicating that LPs developed using CDs(5 wt%)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs maintain excellent stability over time. Thus, CDs(5 wt%)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs can be effectively utilized in cheiloscopy applications [112,113].

To expand the practical applications of the developed NCs in AC technology, the optimized CDs(5 wt%)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs are incorporated into security ink. As illustrated in Fig. 13 a, the developed images on various surfaces (plastic, painted wood, currency, paper, aluminum foil, glass, metal sheet) are achieved using a simple brush application and are visualized under 365 nm UV light. The resulting AC patterns on different substrates exhibit a vivid red colour under 365 nm UV light, while remaining invisible under normal daylight. By incorporating these patterns into security documents with unique designs and text, they serve as optical sensors for detecting authenticity and verifying the integrity of various security documents. This approach shows significant potential in mitigating the risk of forgery and counterfeiting for vital national or international documents like currency, passports, national ID cards, certificates, economic contracts, and sensitive papers. Therefore, this authentication security technology, which exhibits ultra-bright fluorescence emission for the first time, holds significant promise as an effective optical sensor for verifying the originality and authenticity of security documents [114]. The long-term storage stability of CDs(5 wt%)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs AC inks is assessed by using the AC pattern as a test specimen over a period of 270 days under ambient conditions, as illustrated in Fig. 13b. A consistent level of fluorescent intensity is maintained for up to 150 days, after which a slight decrease to 75 % intensity is observed (Fig. 13b1). Additionally, the fluorescence stability of the ink is tested under UV treatment (36 mW/cm2) for various durations, as shown in Fig. 13c. The fluorescent intensity remained stable from 30 to 120 min, followed by a decrease to 79.63 % intensity (Fig. 13c1), indicating excellent UV resistance of the AC tags. Furthermore, Fig. 13d illustrates the treatment of AC tags under various pH conditions (pH ∼3–13/15 min). The highest intensity is observed at pH ∼3, with a subsequent intensity drop (74.1 %) at pH ∼13 [115–117] (Fig. 13d1).
Note: EL spectra of the manufactured w-LED, CIE chromaticity coordinates for white light. The Ra and R1 ∼R14 comparison between commercial and the fabricated LEDs is shown in Fig. S14.

In conclusion, we have devised a straightforward and eco-friendly approach to augment the luminescent properties of ZAO:Eu3+ NPs by combining them with CDs to create CDs@ZAO:Eu3+ NCs. Despite minimal impact on phase structure and morphology, the emission intensity of CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs enhanced by 12.5 folds compared to ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs. The augmentation in luminescence is likely ascribed to electron capture by CDs, followed by the energy transfer between CDs and luminescent Eu3+. This study not only introduces a synthesis pathway for aluminates with a 3D structure but also provides a straightforward and practical method to improve the luminescence properties of nanomaterials doped with rare earth ions. The mechanism behind luminescence enhancement likely involves FRET between CDs and luminescent Eu3+. Furthermore, the stability of the NCs over CDs is thoroughly examined. For potential applications in optical thermometry or temperature sensing, the CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs exhibited a high Ea value of 0.27 eV and a remarkable thermal stability of 90 % with the relative sensitivity was 1.36 %, at 300 K. Additionally, FPs images developed using CDs(5 wt %)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs on various surfaces are showcased. The distinguishing features of LFPs at levels I-III are readily identifiable, facilitating individual identification. Poroscopic investigation enable comparison between male and female prints to ascertain individual traits. Additionally, the potential applications of CDs(5 wt%)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs in AC ink, demonstrated through brush mode, exhibit ultra-bright fluorescence emission, suggesting their efficacy as optical sensors for verifying the authenticity of security documents through photo-sensing.
